translation.content: A lot can happen in 20 years. That's especially true in dance music, where movements and trends bubble up, bounce off one another and dissipate at startling pace. It takes conviction, dynamism and an expansive record collection to stay the course. So there's an obvious reason why Margaret Dygas and Sonja Moonear have remained durable underground favourites over the past two decades. When they step into the booth, either solo or as partners, an assured calm floods the dance floor. You know you're in the safest, sleekest hands imaginable. Our fourth RA.999, recorded live at fabric's 25th birthday celebrations, is a testament to exactly that. The high-tempo and abundantly groovy set sees both Dygas and Moonear return to the series: Moonear had previously contributed RA.520, while Dygas logged just the fourth mix in the RA Podcast's history. It wasn't meant to see the light of day. We're glad it has. The pair also gave rare interviews, reflecting on the evolution of DJing and a shared connection that dates back to 2007. It's one of the most extensive, and perhaps even heartfelt, conversations we've run to accompany a Podcast: spanning everything from the boundlessly influential minimal moment and what makes the grade for their coveted record bags, through to the trade-offs of technology and value of discretion in an age of encroachment. "I felt excited and lucky to be invited so early in what I now see as a much longer journey," wrote Dygas. "Music holds memories in its frequencies, and the right track can transport you instantly to a past version of yourself. That's powerful. That's the kind of power I respect." Amen. – Gabriel Szatan Can you tell us the story behind this mix, and what do you hope listeners take away from it? Margaret Dygas: The story is pretty simple. We were asked to play together, and we thought, why not just keep it spontaneous? So we didn't announce anything, and showed up with open ears and open hearts. That's how we both feel b2bs work best: something that happens naturally, when both people feel it. We both love playing solo, but, when the vibe is right, playing with friends can be magic. And that day at fabric, the vibe was right. Sonja was originally supposed to play after me, but she came early, and we ended up sharing one of the most joyful sets either of us had played in a while. We just had fun. Hopefully, you can feel that. Sonja Moonear: As a snapshot of a five-hour jam session that happened during fabric's 25th anniversary celebration, I guess it's recommended for listeners to picture themselves going down the fabric stairs at noon on a rainy Sunday in mid-October, pushing the wooden doors to reveal a massive, vibing communion on the dance floor. As both Margaret and I prefer the spontaneous b2b action, we chose to decide on the night if we felt like it or not. Playing together requires several alignments: from simple technical requirements—functioning setup and proper sound system, plus enough space in the booth for two ladies and their record bags—to a fitting atmosphere on both the dance floor and between us. If one doesn't feel right, it's uncomfortable for everyone present to force it. That particular night, we came down to Room 1 shortly before noon. The party was already at its 13th hour, and the whole club was bouncing in basses, smiles and sweat. If you listen carefully, you can hear the crowd reactions in the back, during the few breakdown moments. You've played together several times. What do you enjoy about each other's style? And are there any memories from overlapping moments in the booth that leap out? SM: What I remember hopefully correctly is that the first time we played together also happened completely spontaneously during the traditional Mayday celebration at Club der Visionäre, in either 2016 or 2017. Margaret always plays the first part of the day, setting the perfect tone to a never-ending party. In general, I take over the last part of it. That year, I kept happily going and around 2 PM the next day, Margaret came down to pick up her record bags. She proposed to join, which I was so happy about. We jammed for another five or so hours, laughing and wiggling like two kids on their favourite playground. The counter example came during the official Sonus afterparty in 2017. I was set to play after Margaret, but promoters and friends had been pushing the idea of a b2b on us for weeks. On the day, Margaret was playing one of her notorious, brilliant, brain-tickling sets, and towards the second half of it, she nodded at me to join her. We never spoke about it, but I think I can easily say we both felt a bit obliged and it ended up exactly as we thought: between my vinyl and her CDJs, with an unstable and ungrounded setup, the music kept jumping and sounded so bad. I completely disrupted the vibe and the whole thing became very uncomfortable. After that, both our agents kept receiving a fair amount of b2b requests and we directly knew we shouldn't do it again, unless everything is spot on. I've been a fan of Margaret since I first listened to her play, when we met in 2007. It was for a City Fox party in Zürich. I was actually pregnant and that was one of my last sets before a long motherhood break. We had dinner together at the hotel and clicked on a human level. She has this particular gift of creating a musical atmosphere that is captivating and powerful, while playing tracks that are stripped down, sometimes with long "no straight beats" moments and in general without snare drums at all. When we play, we listen and follow while still proposing different styles, but always with a logical link: be it a bassline, instrument or just a vocal that rhymes with the next one. I think we share a similar platonic love, appreciation and respect for this music. When everything comes together just right, it's a real joy to do it. MD: We haven't kept count—maybe someone out there has a spreadsheet going?—but what I do know is, if it didn't feel right, we wouldn't be sharing this mix. We ended up playing five hours, which is when the real fun begins; enough time to forget the clock and get lost in the story. What I love about playing with Sonja is how effortless it feels. There's no pressure to match each other or show off. We both just do our thing and listen closely. Sometimes it's like sending each other musical inside jokes; sometimes we surprise each other; sometimes we just grin in the booth because the energy feels that good. The sound at fabric was spot on, when you can hear your records exactly as they're meant to be heard, it's like everything else fades out and you're just being. The portion of this fabric recording begins faster than your usual pace in order to keep momentum from the previous act going. Many DJs increase the tempo as they play, so how do you approach bringing things down to a simmer without sacrificing energy? MD: Ah yes, the BPM handoff. That day was definitely one of those moments. I remember walking into Room 1 and thinking: Wow, okay… we're flying! It was fast. The first hour we were tuning into that existing energy without causing a full-system shock. I've learnt over the years that cutting the tempo too sharply can lead to one thing: a very empty dance floor. It's all about respecting the frequency people are already moving in. We're all vibrating—literally—and music, being vibration itself, plays with that. So out of respect for the vibe before and for the dancers fully immersed in it, we didn't force anything. Instead, Sonja and I gently shifted things over time, like sneaking in a plot twist without the crowd noticing. That's the beauty of playing longer, you have space to gradually find your own rhythm while keeping people on the journey with you. In short: no sudden moves. We're DJs, not emergency brakes! [Laughs] SM: It always depends on the situation, you know. Is it a small venue or a big festival? Do I have two hours or five, and is the event lasting for six or 20 hours? One can say there are two types of DJs: the ones that stick to their sound and rightfully don't compromise, and the others that look at the night as an ensemble. I'm more geared towards the latter. I see the event as a whole, and will try to picture my contribution to it while preparing my bag. Before I confirm a gig, I need to have these details. I won't prepare the same way if I play a long set in a small venue with just one or two other DJs than if it's a 90-minute slot on a multi-stage festival, taking over after someone that is known for playing over 133 BPM dark electro, for example. Maybe I'd rather not confirm that one to begin with! [Laughs] My standard cruising speed tends to be at 128 BPM though. That morning, we took over after Bicep and the pace was strong. In such a situation of a bouncy packed dance floor, it was logical to keep it as such and gradually bring the tempo down. We knew we had the time for it. This segment's still quite fast and certainly more techno than we're used to. But we loved it! This marks a return to the RA Podcast for both of you: Sonja, your mix came out in 2016, while Margaret, you were our fourth-ever mix way back in 2006. How would you say your record bags and approach to DJing has evolved since those debut editions? SM: I feel as if I'm not the best to speak about what I do. In general, I don't think much has changed since I started DJing in 1995, apart from the process of looking for music, as there are fewer physical record stores today than 30 years ago. But the obsession stays the same: listening to music, discovering new and old records, trying to manage to fit the right selection in these bags before taking off to the next gig with still pretty much unsuccessful results. Sometimes one never learns. RA.520 specifically was recorded at home with the idea of a mix that'll be heard online, while this mix was never meant to see the light out of fabric's Room 1. MD: Believe it or not, I remember that day back in 2006. I felt excited and lucky to be invited so early in what I now see as a much longer journey. Keeping out of the spotlight has become my comfort zone. That's where I feel the magic still lives. Everything is shared instantly and publicly, there's no time for things to breathe, to be held, to be special. That rare record you spent weeks digging for? Played once in the right setting, it could feel like gold. But now, it's posted, ID'd, reposted, and algorithm'd to death within hours. I think that's why we've seen a rise in "no phones" parties—people want to feel the moment, not just document it. As for the actual record bags, I tend to switch them up each time I play, with a few permanent residents that I'm loving at the time (at least until social media gives them too much exposure and I have to retire them early). Some of those favourites are just sitting on my shelf, waiting for a new generation to be ready. I always say: one day they'll be back in Auntie Mar-D's bag for another ride down the rabbit hole. A major shift since 2006 has been the evolution of formats, especially CDJs. When I started, many of us were still recording mixes on cassette tapes. Some were even splicing transitions with nail polish and sticky tape! RA.004 wasn't quite that raw, but it definitely wasn't glued together in a DAW either. The human touch still mattered. It still does. The early CDJ era brought its own kind of magic. I remember travelling with massive CD wallets: 300-plus discs, easy. It opened up so much more room to explore genre-wise. I was discovering new styles constantly, and with that came a deep refusal to box myself in, even though some still try. Just because someone once heard me warming up at Visionäre doesn’t mean I've signed a lifelong contract to that BPM or mood. That's not how music or people work. I started during the minimal moment, and I still love a lot of that sound. But I keep those records not because they're minimal, but because they're good. Playing them again feels like time travel. Music holds memories in its frequencies, and the right track can transport you instantly to a past version of yourself. That's powerful. That's the kind of power I respect. Another thing I loved about that period was being able to test friends' music—fresh out of their studios, sometimes still warm on the CD. That kind of exchange was priceless. We'd play unreleased tracks no one else had, and because there was no Instagram or YouTube flooding the scene, you had to be there to experience it. No posts, no IDs, no replays. Just you, the music and that night. And if you missed it? Well… you snooze you lose, bruv. Of course, there's another side to all this change. Digital formats brought huge convenience, and with that, a certain kind of laziness crept in too. Not blaming anyone, it's just the way things go when tech advances. Time got shorter, inboxes got longer and everyone had to juggle a bit more. Record shops closed, USBs replaced crates and for vinyl lovers like me, it became a balancing act. Not all venues treat vinyl seriously anymore—bad setups, careless monitoring, unstable booths. I've been in situations where I flew hours with a full bag, only to have every record skip. That's not just frustrating, it's heartbreaking. Can you share a mix that's particularly special to you? What makes it special? MD: Yes! One that always lifts me up is Man Or The Music (Vol.1) by J. PERIOD. If you're ever having a rough day, if someone upsets you or things just feel off, I recommend putting this on. It puts the spring right back in your step. What makes it special? It's Michael Jackson, the one and only, who shaped my love for music from a very young age. There's no other musician quite like him, he belongs to his own category. He had me hooked the first time I heard his vocal and I've loved him ever since. No one will ever take his place, no matter how many albums, #1s or downloads they get. All of that's just numbers. He is Neo. There is someone else who touches me in a similarly deep way, though in a completely different universe: Nik Bärtsch. When I saw Nik's band, Ronin, live at Houghton, I was in awe. I felt that same intensity, that rare level of devotion and precision that comes from living inside your craft. The kind of obsession that moves you, that stays with you, that's felt even in silence. Very different spectrums but both have shaped me. Both remind me why I care so much. SM: I will shamefully acknowledge that I never listen to mixes at home. I'd much rather go to listen to DJs mixing at an event. When I listen to music at home, it's either albums or songs on physical media, or songs when I'm searching for new music online. On the very rare occasions I have a little party gathering at home and no one is actually playing songs, I have these two favourite recordings of Zip and Rhadoo that each last over seven hours. Each time this happens, I discover new magic moments I hadn't heard previously. It's always a surprise. In a world where technology is rapidly changing the way we DJ, how do you see the future of DJing and mixing evolving? SM: I'm not a great fortune teller, so these are just my two cents. My feeling is that there has always been an evolution. You just need to adapt to it. Back in the days you could arrive at a venue that had belt-driven Stanton turntables, or a DJ mixer that had no channel EQs, for example. Of course the evolution in technology is faster these days and the constant run towards the newest, fast and best products released by manufacturers doesn't help. The number of actors is constantly rising and everyone wants their fair chunk of the cake. I somehow enjoy discovering new technologies as well when they are worth it, but in general I prefer to stick to the well-functioning equipment that fits the way I DJ. This is when I can fully focus on actually DJing, instead of trying to fix the light plastic tonearm of an SL-1200MK7 set on a camping table in the middle of a shaky stage. The evolution I wish to see would be into perfecting DJ booths in general, rather than seeing newer and bigger twinkling CDJs and controllers I don't have a use for. MD: Whatever I think probably doesn't matter much. That train has already left the station and it's running the latest CDJs with screens bigger than my laptop. I'm doing my best to stay grounded and not get dizzy keeping up with every new tech update. They say, "if you can’t beat them, join them," and I've already joined, to the extent that I'm comfortable. Sometimes I feel like E.T. scrolling through touchscreen menus whispering, "Home… home…" under my breath. [Laughs] If you do want to go deeper into where we're headed, look up Rudolf Steiner. Some clues might be there. Technically, I'm speaking here not just for myself, but for a lot of vinyl lovers: we'd love to see more care from venues and promoters. Proper setups. Working equipment. Great sound. A vibe that respects the music, whatever format it's on. More heart, less business. Because at the end of the day, everyone will make their money, but if the vibe's not right, no one goes home feeling like they were part of something special. For me, I'd rather we spend less time chasing buttons and more time perfecting what we already have. Beyond that, everyone should do what they do best. The goal should always be: "Wow, that was amazing. What a space, what a vibe, what a tribe. I want to come back again." Not, "That was crowded, expensive and the sound was meh." Tech is not the enemy. It can be brilliant. Watching this, I think: that's beautiful. That's creativity. And no chance he's riding a bike making strangers dance with two Technics and a crate of vinyl on his handlebars. So yes, tech can open doors, but nothing will ever replace the feeling of mixing two records on a sound system with a cheeky handmade rotary mixer and a crowd tuned into the same wavelength. Anyone can rent two speakers and borrow some CDJs, but we're already overdue for a shift in intention. A little step back in why we gather, and what we're actually here to share. That takes intention. That takes care. And you feel it, just like you can feel the difference between a moment made for the algorithm, and one made for the soul.
translation.blurb: Part four of RA.999: the sound of shared history, courtesy of two legends of minimal house in full flow.
